SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by comparative experiments between TRANSFER, WAC and SIM-WAP on REFERIT and SAIAPR using GoogleNet features and word2vec, and hyperparameters are reported. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and complementary metrics (hit@k and embedding cosine), but the reduced 159-word vocabulary, lack of mention of code release, and no reported statistical significance testing leave reproducibility and strength of some claims somewhat underconstrained. Mathematical components appear straightforward (ridge regression, per-word predictors) and consistent with the experiments, and the authors acknowledge important limitations (distributional vs referential mismatch). Overall the evidence supports the conclusions but a few methodological details and significance analyses are missing. | B(4): The paper states clear hypotheses about complementarity between distributional and referential signals and supports them with experiments comparing TRANSFER, WAC and SIM-WAP on standard datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR) using sensible metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine), showing combined models improve accuracy. There are no complex mathematical claims—ridge regression and per-word classifiers are standard and correctly applied—but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and code release, so some empirical robustness checks appear missing. Overall the experimental methodology and baselines are appropriate and the conclusions (including acknowledged limitations about distributional vs referential mismatch) are justified by the presented results, with only minor concerns about additional significance analyses and broader-scale validation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier ML/NLP interests: vision-and-language object naming and cross-modal transfer using standard datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR, ImageNet) and sensible baselines (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP). Methods and evaluation use community-expected practices (hit@k, embedding-based semantic metrics, pretrained GoogleNet features) and hyperparameters are reported, and the work explicitly engages relevant prior work and limitations (reduced 159-word vocabulary). From a reproducibility and audience perspective it addresses the right community and problems for a top conference, with only minor practical concerns (code release not mentioned) that do not affect topical fit. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier vision-and-language and NLP/ML venues: it tackles object naming and referring expressions by combining distributional (word2vec) and referential (visual) signals, proposes novel model variants (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP), and evaluates on standard datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR) with accepted metrics (hit@k, embedding distances). The formulation, baselines, and analysis of when lexical distributional cues complement visual classifiers—including zero-shot, singular/plural, and semantic vs. referential failure modes—are directly relevant to the conference audience and build on existing literature in a way that will interest both NLP and multimodal ML researchers.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a logical progression from introduction through methods and experiments, and its main contributions are stated early and repeated in the summary. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. Writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage, though the summary notes some potential ambiguities (e.g., the reduced 159-word vocabulary and lack of mention of released code) that could be clarified for reproducibility and ease of reading. | B(4): From the provided summary the paper is well-organized and follows a clear flow from problem motivation through methods (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) to experiments and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, while figures/tables and concrete examples (e.g., singular/plural behavior, taxonomic vs attribute naming) are informative. Writing quality is reported as good with few grammatical issues, though the presentation could better highlight limitations (vocabulary reduction, dataset scale, and code availability) earlier and more explicitly for readers interested in reproduction. Overall clarity is high with only minor presentation improvements suggested.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its proposed SIM-WAP against two well-motivated baselines (WAC and TRANSFER) using the same visual features, datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR) and consistent metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine), which supports fair comparisons. The authors analyze where each model succeeds/fails, acknowledge limitations and when prior approaches (e.g., transfer mapping) produce comparable semantic coherence, and discuss related work including Ordonez et al. The main shortcoming is scale and vocabulary reductions (159 entry-level names) and no code release mentioned, which leaves open whether additional recent or larger-scale baselines might change conclusions—hence a near-comprehensive but not fully exhaustive comparison. | B(4): The paper compares its proposed SIM-WAP to sensible, recent baselines (WAC and TRANSFER) that capture the two primary modelling paradigms, and evaluates all methods on the same datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR) with consistent metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine). The authors clearly analyze where each method succeeds or fails (semantic coherence vs fine-grained distinctions), acknowledge when prior approaches scale differently (e.g. Ordonez et al., 2016) and discuss limitations of distributional signals. Given the thorough related-work discussion and fair empirical setup, but the reduced 159-word vocabulary and smaller-scale experiments noted by the authors, I rate the comparisons strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its proposed SIM-WAP against two well-motivated baselines (WAC and TRANSFER) using the same visual features, datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR) and consistent metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine), which supports fair comparisons. The authors analyze where each model succeeds/fails, acknowledge limitations and when prior approaches (e.g., transfer mapping) produce comparable semantic coherence, and discuss related work including Ordonez et al. The main shortcoming is scale and vocabulary reductions (159 entry-level names) and no code release mentioned, which leaves open whether additional recent or larger-scale baselines might change conclusions—hence a near-comprehensive but not fully exhaustive comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its proposed SIM-WAP to sensible, recent baselines (WAC and TRANSFER) that capture the two primary modelling paradigms, and evaluates all methods on the same datasets (REFERIT, SAIAPR) with consistent metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine). The authors clearly analyze where each method succeeds or fails (semantic coherence vs fine-grained distinctions), acknowledge when prior approaches scale differently (e.g. Ordonez et al., 2016) and discuss limitations of distributional signals. Given the thorough related-work discussion and fair empirical setup, but the reduced 159-word vocabulary and smaller-scale experiments noted by the authors, I rate the comparisons strong but not fully comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a meaningful problem—grounding lexical distributional signals with visual referential evidence for object naming—and shows that combining these signals (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) yields complementary benefits and improved zero-shot generalization. The SIM-WAP finding (leveraging lexical similarity to train per-word predictors) is practically useful and could change how researchers design zero-shot naming systems, but the contribution is limited by a reduced 159-word vocabulary, relatively small-scale evaluation, and no mention of released code. Given these strengths and constraints, the work is a solid, usable contribution with moderate impact rather than a field-transforming advance. | B(3): The paper tackles an important issue—how to combine distributional and referential signals for object naming—and provides concrete evidence that these sources are complementary (improved hit@k and semantically coherent outputs from TRANSFER vs fine-grained distinctions from WAC), which will inform researchers working on referring-expression generation and grounded lexical learning. The SIM-WAP idea (similarity-weighted per-word predictors) and its zero-shot advantages are a practically useful insight that could guide follow-up models. However, the impact is moderated by scope limitations (small vocabulary of 159 entry-level names, dataset scale) and the acknowledged mismatch between distributional similarity and referential meaning, so the work is more likely to spur incremental follow-up studies than to fundamentally redirect the field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs targeted experiments comparing TRANSFER, WAC and SIM-WAP on REFERIT and SAIAPR using GoogleNet features and standard metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine) and reports hyperparameters and several insightful analyses (semantic vs. fine-grained errors, zero-shot behavior). It also acknowledges limitations (reduced 159-word vocabulary, distributional vs. referential mismatch) and situates results in related work. However, important reproducibility aids are missing (no code availability or explicit data splits mentioned), and additional ablations/statistical tests (e.g., significance, more sensitivity analyses around vocabulary size and training data) are needed to fully support some claims. | B(4): The experiments are well-structured and compare three principled models (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) across multiple datasets (ReferIt, SAIAPR) with both naming and zero-shot evaluations, using sensible metrics (hit@k and embedding distances) and targeted analyses (e.g., singular/plural behavior, semantic coherence vs fine-grained confusions). The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses failure modes and the limitation of a reduced 159-word vocabulary and distributional–referential mismatch, and the related work coverage is thorough. Missing, however, are larger-scale evaluations (beyond the reduced vocabulary) and publicly available code to maximize reproducibility and ease sensitivity/ablation extensions, so the empirical support is strong but not exhaustive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs targeted experiments comparing TRANSFER, WAC and SIM-WAP on REFERIT and SAIAPR using GoogleNet features and standard metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine) and reports hyperparameters and several insightful analyses (semantic vs. fine-grained errors, zero-shot behavior). It also acknowledges limitations (reduced 159-word vocabulary, distributional vs. referential mismatch) and situates results in related work. However, important reproducibility aids are missing (no code availability or explicit data splits mentioned), and additional ablations/statistical tests (e.g., significance, more sensitivity analyses around vocabulary size and training data) are needed to fully support some claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are well-structured and compare three principled models (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) across multiple datasets (ReferIt, SAIAPR) with both naming and zero-shot evaluations, using sensible metrics (hit@k and embedding distances) and targeted analyses (e.g., singular/plural behavior, semantic coherence vs fine-grained confusions). The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses failure modes and the limitation of a reduced 159-word vocabulary and distributional–referential mismatch, and the related work coverage is thorough. Missing, however, are larger-scale evaluations (beyond the reduced vocabulary) and publicly available code to maximize reproducibility and ease sensitivity/ablation extensions, so the empirical support is strong but not exhaustive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents a moderately novel combination of ideas rather than a radical new paradigm: it systematically compares cross-modal transfer (TRANSFER), per-word visual classifiers (WAC), and a similarity-weighted per-word predictor (SIM-WAP) that exploits distributional (word2vec) similarity as soft labels. Framing and empirically evaluating SIM-WAP as a way to inject lexical similarity into per-word predictors is a non-trivial extension beyond vanilla WAC/transfer, and the reported zero-shot/generalization gains would be informative to experts. However, the core components (ridge mapping into word2vec space, per-word classifiers, and using distributional similarities) are all known techniques, and the work mainly integrates them on a modest-sized vocabulary and datasets, so the overall novelty is incremental rather than highly original. | B(4): The paper offers a notable, though not revolutionary, novelty by proposing SIM-WAP (similarity-weighted per-word predictors) and systematically studying the complementarity of distributional vs referential signals for naming—going beyond prior work that used either visual classifiers or cross-modal mapping alone. The formulation of per-word predictors trained with real-valued lexical similarity labels and the analysis contrasting semantic coherence (TRANSFER) with fine-grained visual sensitivity (WAC) constitutes a non-trivial methodological extension and is clearly differentiated from related efforts (e.g., Ordonez et al.). An expert would find the empirical and conceptual distinctions informative, even if the overall problem and components build on well-established techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a moderately novel combination of ideas rather than a radical new paradigm: it systematically compares cross-modal transfer (TRANSFER), per-word visual classifiers (WAC), and a similarity-weighted per-word predictor (SIM-WAP) that exploits distributional (word2vec) similarity as soft labels. Framing and empirically evaluating SIM-WAP as a way to inject lexical similarity into per-word predictors is a non-trivial extension beyond vanilla WAC/transfer, and the reported zero-shot/generalization gains would be informative to experts. However, the core components (ridge mapping into word2vec space, per-word classifiers, and using distributional similarities) are all known techniques, and the work mainly integrates them on a modest-sized vocabulary and datasets, so the overall novelty is incremental rather than highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable, though not revolutionary, novelty by proposing SIM-WAP (similarity-weighted per-word predictors) and systematically studying the complementarity of distributional vs referential signals for naming—going beyond prior work that used either visual classifiers or cross-modal mapping alone. The formulation of per-word predictors trained with real-valued lexical similarity labels and the analysis contrasting semantic coherence (TRANSFER) with fine-grained visual sensitivity (WAC) constitutes a non-trivial methodological extension and is clearly differentiated from related efforts (e.g., Ordonez et al.). An expert would find the empirical and conceptual distinctions informative, even if the overall problem and components build on well-established techniques.

main_claims: Combining distributional (word embeddings) and referential (visual) information improves object naming accuracy compared to individual models, indicating complementary strengths.

A direct cross-modal mapping (TRANSFER) into a distributional space produces semantically coherent top-n predictions but can fail to make fine-grained distinctions, while visual word classifiers (WAC) react to subtle visual differences but sometimes produce taxonomically distant terms.

The SIM-WAP model (similarity-weighted per-word predictors) generalizes better in zero-shot naming, especially for singular/plural cases, by exploiting lexical similarity signals when training per-word predictors.
_summary.methodology: Compare three models for object naming—TRANSFER (ridge-regression mapping of visual features to word2vec vectors), WAC (visual binary classifiers per word), and SIM-WAP (per-word predictors trained with real-valued similarity labels derived from word2vec)—using GoogleNet visual features augmented with region features and word2vec embeddings, and evaluate standalone and combined models on naming and zero-shot tasks.
metrics: cosine distance between word2vec embeddings of top-k outputs
limitations: Distributional lexical similarity does not always align with referential meaning and can mislead naming decisions.
Cross-modal transfer can fail to make desired fine-grained distinctions despite producing semantically coherent outputs.
Experiments use a reduced vocabulary (159 entry-level names) and are on a smaller scale than some related work (Ordonez et al., 2016).

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in machine learning and several years building and evaluating vision-and-language models in industry and academia. Experienced in experimental design, statistical analysis, open-sourcing code, and running large-scale benchmark experiments.
traits: insists on clear baselines, ablations, and statistical significance for claimed improvements
looks for reproducibility: hyperparameters, training details, data splits, and code availability
values principled evaluation metrics and error analysis that reveal when models fail
notices implementation shortcuts (e.g., data leakage, pretraining confounds) and requests controls
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological soundness and reproducibility, recommending experiments and checks needed to support empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Vision-and-Language Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor working on referring expressions, grounded language learning, and multimodal semantics, with a track record of bridging computational methods and linguistic/psychological insights. Familiar with prior work on cross-modal mappings, referential models, and lexical semantics.
traits: evaluates novelty and how the paper advances the field relative to existing literature
values insights about when and why different information sources (distributional vs referential) complement each other
looks for practical implications for downstream tasks (e.g., referring expression generation, assistive tech) and limitations in real-world use
appreciates linguistic analysis (e.g., singular/plural behavior, taxonomic vs attribute-based naming) and qualitative examples
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's conceptual contribution and broader significance, suggesting connections to related work and directions to increase impact.
